summary: This lesson teaches how the subjunctive mood is used to form commands/orders. With negative commands and formal/plural commands, the subjunctive is used. For positive informal commands, a shortcut exists: drop the -s from the tú form. Object pronouns attach to the end of positive commands but precede negative commands.

teachings.summary: Commands in Spanish use the subjunctive mood in most cases: all negative commands, all formal commands, and all plural commands. The subjunctive gives the command form. For positive informal (tú) commands, a shortcut is used instead: drop the -s from the present tense tú form.
teachings.rule: Negative commands (all persons): use subjunctive. Formal commands: use subjunctive. Plural commands: use subjunctive. Positive informal commands: drop -s from present tú form (compras -> compra, comes -> come).

examples.spanish: No se lo des a él.

teachings.summary: With positive commands, object pronouns (me, lo, se, etc.) attach to the end of the verb as one word. With negative commands, pronouns go before the verb in their normal position.
teachings.rule: Positive command: verb + pronoun(s) as one word (e.g., espérenme, cómalo, cómpramelo). Negative command: no + pronoun(s) + verb (e.g., no lo coma, no los esperen).
teachings.notes: The pronoun placement with positive commands helps the ear recognize it as a command, since the vowel switch alone is a subtle change.

teachings.summary: For positive informal (tú) commands only, instead of using the subjunctive, Spanish drops the -s from the tú present tense form. This is a shortcut because telling people informally what to do is extremely common.
teachings.rule: compras -> compra (Buy!), comes -> come (Eat!), olvidas -> olvida (Forget!). The result looks identical to the he/she/it form but is derived by dropping -s from tú.

examples.english: Don't sell it. (informal -- back to subjunctive)

teachings.notes: Only applies to POSITIVE INFORMAL commands. Negative informal commands still use the subjunctive (no lo comas, not *no lo come).

teachings.summary: Olvidar means to forget. It needs an object (what is forgotten). If no object is stated, use the reflexive olvidarse (me olvido). Related to English 'oblivion'.
teachings.notes: Related to English 'oblivion'. The verb doesn't like to be alone -- needs an object or reflexive.

teachings.conceptName: Callar / callarse (to quiet / to shut up)
teachings.summary: Callar means to quiet; callarse means to shut up (reflexive). Not polite.
teachings.notes: Cállate uses the informal positive shortcut. Cállense uses the subjunctive because it's plural.
